core_takeaway: Agent_1 looks advantaged over Agent_3 because its opening Jewel-first allocation became the shared baseline and the Pareto-check rule made deviation hard; however, the advantage is not purely positional because Agent_2, speaking second, tied Agent_1, and Agent_3's lower utility came from a lower-valued accepted bundle plus the round-2 discount.
why_or_counterexample: Earlier position beat last because the first message framed Jewel as nonnegotiable while giving Agent_2 and Agent_3 enough value to accept the baseline. By round 2, the raw votes accepted Agent_1's baseline and rejected proposals that would destabilize or harm Agent_3. The main caveat is that Agent_2 also finished at 90, so the observed gap reflects preference structure and timing as well as first-position anchoring.

phase_notes.public_discussion: Agent_1 opened with Jewel as the anchor and a complete baseline allocation. Agent_2 and Agent_3 quickly aligned publicly, and round 2 largely repeated that no verified Pareto-improving swap was available.
phase_notes.private_thinking: All six private-thinking entries identify the same baseline as the robust path: Jewel with Agent_1, Apple/Stone with Agent_2, and Quill/Pencil with Agent_3. Agent_1 privately planned to defend Jewel while conceding zero-value non-Jewel items.
phase_notes.formal_proposals: Round 1 included two baseline proposals and one Agent_3-favorable alternative. Round 2, Agent_1 proposed the baseline again while Agent_2 and Agent_3 proposed all non-Jewel items to Agent_2; the tabulated votes and raw votes support Proposal #1/baseline despite the system consensus line mislabeling Proposal #3.
phase_notes.private_voting: Round 1 failed unanimity because Agent_3 preferred the proposal giving Stone plus Quill/Pencil. Round 2 raw votes show all agents accepted Proposal #1/baseline and rejected the all-to-Agent_2 alternatives, leaving Agent_3 at 81 after discount.
phase_notes.reflection: The reflections converge on Jewel as the fixed anchor, lack of Pareto-improving swaps, and non-Jewel distribution as the main friction. They recommend formal Pareto checks and a one-swap rule, which preserve the original baseline.

mechanisms.theme: Opening allocation became the focal baseline
mechanisms.explanation: Agent_1 used the first public turn to define the desired item and a full allocation that gave the other agents their salient bundles. That made later discussion revolve around preserving or verifying departures from Agent_1's baseline.

evidence.quote: I value Jewel above all else (worth 100 to me).
I agree with the Round 1 allocation you two outlined

mechanisms.theme: Pareto-check rule locked in the status quo
mechanisms.explanation: Once the baseline was accepted as the reference point, the group required public proof that any swap would leave no one worse off. That rule protected Agent_1's first allocation because most alternatives either failed to improve anyone or made Agent_3 worse off.

evidence.quote: Pareto-improving: at least one agent strictly better off, and no agent worse off
A swap is only allowed if it is Pareto-improving
Round-2 baseline stays in place unless a credible Pareto-improving offer is publicly proposed and verified.

mechanisms.explanation: The decisive round accepted the proposal matching Agent_1's preferred allocation and rejected the alternatives that moved all non-Jewel items to Agent_2. Agent_3 accepted the baseline because it was their best available discounted outcome among the round-2 proposals.

evidence.quote: Jewel remains with Agent1 (100 utility to me)

mechanisms.theme: Preference structure limits a pure order explanation
mechanisms.explanation: The first-over-last gap is real, but it is partly mechanical: Agent_1 and Agent_2 each had a 100-value target item, while Agent_3's accepted core bundle was worth 90 before discount. The second speaker tied Agent_1, so the evidence supports an early stable anchor more than a first-position-only effect.
